subject: ap-psychology
title: Research Methods — Stress, Locus of Control, and Intelligence
stimulus:
Dr. Dawson is a psychologist who believes that students who read motivational statements before a test will
experience lower levels of anxiety as compared with students who read neutral statements. To examine this
belief, he conducted a study. In the study, he randomly assigned half of the participants to read motivational
statements and the other half to read neutral statements. Immediately after reading the statements, all of the
students completed a survey about their current level of anxiety (1 = low; 8 = high) and then took the test. Dr.
Dawson found statistically significant results that are depicted in the graph.

parts.prompt: State a valid operational definition of the dependent variable in this study.

scoring_points.description: Response must indicate that anxiety is defined by their score on the scale/survey of their  anxiety.

required_elements: The dependent variable can be operationally defined as the score on the anxiety scale.
alternatives.correct_example: The dependent variable can be operationally defined as the score on the anxiety scale.

required_elements: The dependent variable is the score on the scale/survey.
alternatives.correct_example: The dependent variable is the score on the scale/survey.

required_elements: The dependent variable is the anxiety score from 1 to 8 (or 1 to 10).
alternatives.correct_example: The dependent variable is the anxiety score from 1 to 8 (or 1 to 10).

wrong_examples: The operational definition is the level of anxiety.
The operational definition is the anxiety score.

parts.prompt: Identify the control group in this study.

scoring_points.description: Response must indicate that the control group is the group that reads the neutral  statements.

required_elements: The control group is the group that reads neutral statements.
alternatives.correct_example: The control group is the group that reads neutral statements.

required_elements: The control group is the group that does not read positive statements.
alternatives.correct_example: The control group is the group that does not read positive statements.

wrong_examples: The control group is the group that reads the positive statements.
The control group is the group that does not read the neutral statements.

parts.prompt: Explain why the study would be considered an experiment.

scoring_points.description: Response must indicate that the study is experimental because an independent variable is  manipulated, and/or the participants were randomly assigned to the groups in the study.

required_elements: The study is experimental because the students are randomly assigned to two conditions.
alternatives.correct_example: The study is experimental because the students are randomly assigned to two conditions.

required_elements: The study is an experiment because a variable was manipulated.
alternatives.correct_example: The study is an experiment because a variable was manipulated.

wrong_examples: The study is experimental because they were randomly chosen to participate.
The study is experimental because it tests a hypothesis.

parts.prompt: Explain how the results of the study do or do not support Dr. Dawson’s hypothesis.

scoring_points.description: Response must indicate that Dr. Dawson’s hypothesis is supported because either the  motivational statement group had lower anxiety or the neutral statement group had  higher anxiety.

required_elements: Dr. Dawson’s hypothesis is supported because the motivational group had lower anxiety.
alternatives.correct_example: Dr. Dawson’s hypothesis is supported because the motivational group had lower anxiety.

required_elements: Dr. Dawson’s hypothesis is supported because the neutral statement group had higher anxiety.
alternatives.correct_example: Dr. Dawson’s hypothesis is supported because the neutral statement group had higher anxiety.

wrong_examples: The results do not support Dr. Dawson’s hypothesis.
Dr. Dawson’s hypothesis is supported because the motivational group had higher anxiety.
Dr. Dawson’s hypothesis is supported because the motivational group had low anxiety.

parts.prompt: The test Dr. Dawson gave was a vocabulary test. Explain how the following terms might relate to the students who performed poorly on the test in this study. Explain how External locus of control relates to the scenario.

scoring_points.description: Response must indicate that students may perform poorly because they believe that  factors outside of themselves (e.g., fate, luck, destiny, circumstances, other people) are  responsible for their performance.

required_elements: Students who believe that fate determines their scores on the test won’t perform as well.
alternatives.correct_example: Students who believe that fate determines their scores on the test won’t perform as well.

required_elements: Students who believe that the unfairness of their teacher determines their score won’t pass the test.
alternatives.correct_example: Students who believe that the unfairness of their teacher determines their score won’t pass the test.

wrong_examples: Students did badly because the questions were too hard.
Students believe they can control how they do on the test.
The students may think there is nothing they can do, so they perform worse on the test.
The students did poorly on the quiz because they believed they lacked the ability to do

parts.prompt: The test Dr. Dawson gave was a vocabulary test. Explain how the following terms might relate to the students who performed poorly on the test in this study. Explain how Stage one of Selye’s general adaptation syndrome relates to the scenario.
parts.scoring_notes: NOTE: Students are not required to identify the name of the stage in order to earn the point.

scoring_points.description:
Response must indicate that the students’ sympathetic nervous system or some specific  aspect of their sympathetic nervous system is activated which interferes with taking the  test. 

OR 

Response indicates that the students’ fight-or-flight mechanism is activated, which  interferes with them taking the test. 

NOTE: Students are not required to identify the name of the stage in order to earn the  point.

required_elements: Students’ fight or flight response might engage if they perceive the test as being a threat, so they might score lower than if they weren’t stressed.
alternatives.correct_example: Students’ fight or flight response might engage if they perceive the test as being a threat, so they might score lower than if they weren’t stressed.

required_elements: Students’ heart rates increased, and they performed poorly.
alternatives.correct_example: Students’ heart rates increased, and they performed poorly.

wrong_examples: The student was too alarmed or too aroused to perform well on the test.
Students with anxiety might get sick because they’ve resisted stress so much.

parts.prompt: The test Dr. Dawson gave was a vocabulary test. Explain how the following terms might relate to the students who performed poorly on the test in this study. Explain how Crystallized intelligence relates to the scenario.

scoring_points.description: Response must indicate that a lack of crystallized intelligence (e.g., accumulated  knowledge, facts, general information, or vocabulary) could have hindered student  performance on the test.

required_elements: Students who study the wrong vocabulary perform poorly.
alternatives.correct_example: Students who study the wrong vocabulary perform poorly.

required_elements: Students who do not have prior knowledge do poorly on the test.
alternatives.correct_example: Students who do not have prior knowledge do poorly on the test.

wrong_examples: Students may not be able to come up with abstract answers quickly, so they score low on the test.
The memory of a past bad experience on a test makes them perform poorly on the current test.
